perturbation.original_text: Subject in any event to Clause 6.2, the Parties in this Agreement shall not be liable to each other, whether in contract, tort, (including negligence) or otherwise, under this Agreement for any loss of business, loss of products, loss of anticipated or actual profit, loss of goodwill or reputation, loss caused by business interruption or (without prejudice to any heads of loss expressly set out in Clause 6.3) any indirect, special or consequential cost, expense, loss or damage, even if such cost, expense, loss or damage was reasonably foreseeable or might have reasonably been contemplated by either Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject in any event to Clause 6.2, the Parties in this Agreement shall not be liable to each other, whether in contract, tort, (including negligence) or otherwise, under this Agreement for any loss of business, loss of products, loss of anticipated or actual profit, loss of goodwill or reputation, loss caused by business interruption or any indirect, special or consequential cost, expense, loss or damage, even if such cost, expense, loss or damage was reasonably foreseeable or might have reasonably been contemplated by either Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "(without prejudice to any heads of loss expressly set out in Clause 6.3)" eliminates an exception that might allow for specific types of damages to be claimed, potentially contradicting laws that mandate certain remedies be available.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-719(3) states that consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. By removing the 'without prejudice' clause, the contract attempts to create a blanket exclusion of consequential damages which might be deemed unconscionable, particularly in cases of consumer goods or where the exclusion would deprive a party of a minimum adequate remedy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes a key exception, '(without prejudice to any heads of loss expressly set out in Clause 6.3)', potentially leading to a blanket exclusion of consequential damages. The Law Explanation suggests this might be deemed unconscionable, particularly in cases of consumer goods, and could deprive a party of a minimum adequate remedy. Snippet 1 from UCC § 2-719(3) states that 'Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable.' By removing the exception, the modified contract weakens the possibility of specific damage claims, directly contradicting the principle that limitations on consequential damages must not be unconscionable, thus creating a legal contradiction based on a legally mandated consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: To the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, (Your Company Name) makes no representation or warranty as to the satisfactory quality, suitability or fitness for purpose of any Samples and, save as expressly provided otherwise in this Agreement, shall not be liable, in negligence or otherwise, for any damage arising from or in connection with any use made by Petro- Lube of any Samples or any allegation that such use infringes any third party intellectual property rights or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: (Your Company Name) makes no representation or warranty as to the satisfactory quality, suitability or fitness for purpose of any Samples and shall not be liable, in negligence or otherwise, for any damage arising from or in connection with any use made by Petro- Lube of any Samples or any allegation that such use infringes any third party intellectual property rights or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'To the fullest extent permitted by applicable law' attempts to expand the disclaimer of warranties beyond what the law allows, which is illegal. This is compounded by removing 'save as expressly provided otherwise in this Agreement'.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 sets strict requirements for disclaiming warranties. Removing 'To the fullest extent permitted by applicable law' implies an attempt to disclaim warranties even where the law forbids it. By not expressly providing a caveat, there is a risk that implied warranties are being disclaimed improperly.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the phrase 'To the fullest extent permitted by applicable law', which weakens the protection afforded by the original contract. UCC § 2-316, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, addresses the exclusion or modification of warranties. By removing this phrase, the contract seemingly attempts to disclaim warranties beyond what the law allows, potentially violating the UCC. Furthermore, removing 'save as expressly provided otherwise in this Agreement' exacerbates this issue by removing an important caveat. This creates a contradiction because the modified text now implies a broader disclaimer than what is legally permissible.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either Party at any time by giving thirty (30) days prior written notification.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either Party at any time.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'thirty (30) days prior written notification' clause violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in most contracts, especially in States like New Jersey (where Petro-Lube is located). Termination without notice can be seen as acting in bad faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey law on implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: New Jersey law, reflecting the UCC's general principles (N.J.S.A. 12A:1-304), implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Terminating a contract without any prior notice could be interpreted as a breach of this implied covenant, especially if the other party has reasonably relied on the continuation of the agreement. Eliminating the notice period removes a safeguard for the other party's interests.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates a 30-day written notification for termination, which acts as a safeguard. The changed text removes this notification period. The Law Explanation suggests this removal could breach the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, particularly if the other party relied on the agreement's continuation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, N.J. Rev. Stat. § 12A:1-304, states that every contract within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Removing the notice period directly contradicts this obligation because it allows for termination without any consideration for the other party's reliance or planning, thus violating the implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, and the relationship between (Your Company Name) and Petro- Lubricant Testing Laboratories, Inc. under this Agreement, shall in all respects be interpreted in accordance with and governed by the laws of the United States of America and the State of (Your State), and the Parties agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of those courts in relation to any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement (whether based in contract, tort (including negligence) or otherwise).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement, and the relationship between (Your Company Name) and Petro- Lubricant Testing Laboratories, Inc. under this Agreement, shall in all respects be interpreted in accordance with and governed by the laws of (Your State), and the Parties agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of those courts in relation to any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement (whether based in contract, tort (including negligence) or otherwise).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'the laws of the United States of America', the contract attempts to waive federal law and jurisdiction, which could be problematic if the subject matter involves federal regulations or interstate commerce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Supremacy Clause of the U.S. Constitution, Article VI, Clause 2
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supremacy Clause of the U.S. Constitution (Article VI, Clause 2) establishes that the Constitution and federal laws are the supreme law of the land. A contract cannot waive the applicability of federal law. If the agreement implicates federal laws (e.g., environmental regulations, interstate commerce), attempting to exclude U.S. law is unenforceable and creates a legal contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article VI | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article VI Primary tabs All debts contracted and engagements entered into, before the adoption of this Constitution, shall be as valid against the United States under this Constitution, as under the Confederation. This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof; and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding. The Senators and Representatives before mentioned, and the members of the several state legislatures, and all executive and judicial officers, both of the United States and of the several states, shall be bound by oath or affirmation, to support this Constitution; but no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States. ‹ Article V up Article VII › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the application of U.S. federal law, which the Supremacy Clause (Article VI) of the U.S. Constitution, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, establishes as the supreme law of the land. The original text acknowledges both state and federal law, but the changed text omits federal law, thus attempting to override the Constitution's mandate. This creates a direct conflict, as contracts cannot waive the applicability of federal laws where they apply.
